turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Amazon Shareholders:

- The largest shareholder of Amazon is its founder and CEO, Jeff Bezos, who owns around 11% of the company's shares.
- Other major shareholders include investment firms such as Vanguard Group, BlackRock, and State Street, as well as mutual funds and institutional investors.
- As of April 2021, Amazon had approximately 4,545 institutional shareholders.

Amazon Board of Directors:

- The Amazon board of directors consists of ten members, including Jeff Bezos, who is currently the executive chairman of the board.
- Other members of the board include:
  - Andy Jassy, who will replace Bezos as CEO later in 2021.
  - Indra Nooyi, the former CEO of PepsiCo.
  - Daniel Huttenlocher, dean of the MIT Schwarzman College of Computing.
- The board also includes senior Amazon executives, such as David Zapolsky, the company's general counsel.

Amazon Top Management:

- In addition to Jeff Bezos and Andy Jassy, other members of Amazon's top management team include:
  - Brian Olsavsky, the company's CFO.
  - Werner Vogels, the CTO.
  - Jay Carney, the SVP for global corporate affairs.
- Amazon's senior vice presidents are responsible for leading different business units within the company, such as Amazon Web Services, consumer retail, and advertising.

References:

- Amazon Investor Relations: https://ir.aboutamazon.com/
- Amazon Board of Directors: https://www.amazon.com/gp/help/customer/display.html?nodeId=201399360
- Amazon Management Team: https://www.aboutamazon.com/our-leadership

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Amazon Shareholders:

Amazon is a publicly-traded company, which means that it has shareholders who own parts of the company through the purchase of stock. The largest shareholder of Amazon is its founder and long-time CEO, Jeff Bezos, who owns around 11% of the company's shares. Bezos has steadily been selling off some of his shares in recent years but remains the richest person in the world, according to Forbes. Other major shareholders of Amazon include investment firms such as Vanguard Group, BlackRock, and State Street, which hold significant amounts of Amazon stock in their portfolios. Mutual funds and institutional investors also own shares of Amazon, which provides a diversified base of ownership for the company.

Amazon Board of Directors:

The Amazon board of directors is a group of ten individuals who provide oversight and guidance to the company's management team. The board includes Jeff Bezos, who recently stepped down as CEO but remains the executive chairman of the board. Andy Jassy, who currently leads Amazon Web Services, will replace Bezos as CEO later in 2021. Other members of the board include Indra Nooyi, the former CEO of PepsiCo, and Daniel Huttenlocher, dean of the MIT Schwarzman College of Computing. The board also includes Amazon's general counsel, David Zapolsky, as well as five independent directors. The board is responsible for making decisions related to major company initiatives, such as acquisitions, capital expenditures, and executive compensation.

Amazon Top Management:

Amazon's top management team consists of several executives who lead various areas of the company. In addition to Jeff Bezos and soon-to-be CEO Andy Jassy, other members of Amazon's executive team include Brian Olsavsky, who serves as the company's Chief Financial Officer, and Werner Vogels, who is the Chief Technology Officer. Jay Carney is the Senior Vice President for Global Corporate Affairs, in charge of the company's public image. Additionally, Amazon has a team of senior vice presidents who lead different business units within the company, such as Amazon Web Services, consumer retail, and advertising.

References:

Amazon Investor Relations: https://ir.aboutamazon.com/

A report on "Why Is the Founder of Amazon the World’s Richest Person?" (by Investopedia) that talks about Amazon's ownership structure: https://www.investopedia.com/articles/personal-finance/091615/why-founder-amazon-worlds-richest-man.asp

A report on Amazon's Board of Directors (by Harvard Business Review): https://hbr.org/2020/02/a-case-study-of-amazons-board-of-directors
